style_note: A driving, micro-tonal blast of instrumental guitar math rock from Canada's viral masked, polka-dot-clad breakout duo.
A legendary, high-energy cumbia anthem celebrating Mexico's colorful lucha libre wrestling culture with bright horns and infectihorns, swinging beat.
A groundbreaking, heavy electronic track fusing traditional Indigenous powwow vocals and drumming with modern dubstep and hip-hop beats.
A hypnotic piece of Franco-Algerian immigrant pop with a dry, driving drum machine beat.
A sultry, cinematic masterpiece blending traditional Argentine tango with slick, down-tempo electronic trip-hop beats.
An atmospheric, synth-driven pub rock love letter to the vastness of the Australian outback and its ancient history.
The pioneer of Euro electro-swing delivers an infectious blend of 1930s jazz vocals fused with driving modern breakbeats.
Satire with swagger in this massive electropop hit, pairing an infectious, melancholic saxophone loop with bleak lyrics about escaping economic despair through dance.

track_name: I am from Bosnia, take me to America
style_note: A satrical swipe at the American Dream, delivered through high-energy Bosnian 'Balkan Ska' blending hip-hop with frantic accordion riffs.
A baile funk phenomenon that brought the raw, heavy beats of Rio's favelas to global mainstream pop. Anitta is appearing in the 2026 World Cup opening ceremony.
The slow, melancholic sound of Cape Verdean 'Morna' given a Parisian cafe uplift.
DJ and beatmaker, popular on TikTok for fusing hip hop beats over '60s Spanish pop songs.
African beats fusing dance and traditional rhythms using DIY instruments.
The joyous theme song to the 2014 World Cup, from the stars of Ivorian Zouglou Afrobeat.
A hauntingly beautiful folk song that revives the ancient language of the Zadar Arbanasi.
A relentless, riotous 'Tambú-tronic' fusion of Afro-Curaçaoan drums and aggressive EDM / Dancehall beats.
Rousing, dark, melancholy and synth-heavy. A passionate cry for 'freedom, resilience, strong wind in your hair and never ending horizon'.
The sounds of the Ecuadorian rainforest give way to a captivating electronic celebration of water as the lifeblood of the jungle.
Egyptian poet, Abdullah Miniawy, delivers an aching, emotional cry against oppresion in this haunting jazz number, rich with trumpet, cello and saxophone.
The only decent England World Cup song.
Jarvis Cocker reimagines a Brigitte Bardot classic with suave charm and a British wink.
The definitive German-language version of the behemoth of 1980s synth pop.
The king of Ghanaian Highlife with his masterful blend of Afrobeat and soulful vocals.
An infectious blend of Haitian drums and Afro-Caribbean rhythms celebrating the Vodou ocean spirit and ancestral pride.

A heart-breaking tribute to 22-year-old Mahsa Amini, who died while in state custody, leading to widespread protests in Iran. The song, which gained worldwide traction, contains a collage of tweets all starting with the word Baraye, meaning 'for' or 'because of', representing the collective concerns of those who demand radical changes in Iran.
Take a meditative break with the Oud vituoso and this composition, set to the sound of songbirds.
Thunderous Japanese Taiko drum performance; visceral, physical and hypnotic.
Indie, reggae, techno? All of the above, infused with the uniquely distinctive sound of the Levantine Mijwiz.
The warm, infectious sound of Amazigh 'desert blues', conjuring the swaying stride of camels and the vastness of the Sahara.
Just an infectiously bright and joyous slice of Dutch indie-pop/rock.
An absolute bombtrack of New Zealand 'Haka Metal' marrying thrash riffs with the fierce, rhythmic war cries of a traditional Māori Haka.

Dark, ambient Nordic folk utilizing ancient instruments for a ritualistic atmosphere. Grá, meaning 'the grey one' refers to the   wolf as a symbol of the broken bond between humans and the wild, offering a song of reconciliation to heal that ancient rift.
Translating to 'Panama is great, and then some!', this is an infectious dose of late 60s, early 70s Latin salsa jazz.
Meaning 'The Bellbird', this is a delightful instrumental track in which the harp is used to mimic the call of Paraguay's national bird.
Meaning 'Strange Way of Life', this is one of the absolute pillars of Portuguese Fado, capturing the essence of saudade, the Portuguese blend of longing, melancholy, and resignation to fate.
Meaning 'My Beloved', this is one of the pioneering tracks of modern Khaleeji (Persian Gulf) pop
Meaning 'Blessings of God upon the Prophet', this track depicts the 14th Century 'Islamic Marco Polo', Ibn Battuta's pilgrimage to Mecca.
Celtic covers of '70s and '80s punk and new wave classics. Possibly predicting Scotland's performance at the 2026 World Cup ;)
This late-70s gem sees a young Youssou N'Dour weaving soulful Wolof vocals over the poly-rhythmic sound of Senegalese Mbalax.

A juggernaut of the late '90s Kwaito movement, born in the townships of Johannesburg. African percussion, deep basslines, and Mandoza's roaring, unmistakable Zulu vocals became a unifying stadium anthem across South Africa.
The global phenomenon that brought K-pop to the world stage.
Dark, viseral, operatic, epic. A tour de force from the genre-defying Catalan superstar.

Relentless yet hypnotic, this 138 BPM psytrance odyssey celebrates indigenous people of Sápmi, a cultural region that stretches across the northern parts of Norway, Sweden, Finland, and the Kola Peninsula of Russia.
At the forefront of Neue Deutsche Welle (German New Wave). Swiss band Grauzone offer an anxious, driving synth track about wanting to be a polar bear to never have to cry again.
The haunting, soaring, melancholic voice of Emel, the 'Voice of the Tunisian Revolution', recorded minimally during the 2020 Lockdown with a single guitar and a laptop in a quiet Tunisian garden.
A joyous, avant-garde 1974 crossover that takes a traditional Turkish folk song and supercharges it with fuzzy psych-rock.

artist_name: Jorge Drexler with Rueda de Candombe
style_note: The once-banned sound of Afro-Uruguayan Candombe, is a hypnotic blend of acordian and traditional percussion.

track_name: Sen Qaidan Bilasan (How Do You Know)
style_note: A driving space-disco track from Uzbekistan's thriving '80s music scene, reminiscent of Giorgio Moroder or Cerrone.
